mappings.rationale: Directly covers the entire history of medicine from ancient through modern times, which is the central topic of section A.
Epidemiology is a core medical discipline related to public health (B.2.d), bacteriology (A.2.c), and the study of disease transmission (A.2.e).
Pandemics relate to the history of infectious disease, bacteriology (A.2.c), immunology (A.3.b), and tropical medicine (A.3.d.iii).
Plague is a historically significant disease illustrating the evolution of medicine from ancient practices through the establishment of bacteriology.
Sections A.2.c (bacteriology), A.3.a (antibiotics), and A.3.b (immunology) all address the medical response to infectious disease.
HIV/AIDS represents a major 20th-century medical challenge, relevant to immunology (A.3.b) and tropical medicine (A.3.d.iii).
Section A.3.b.ii covers vaccines for viral diseases including yellow fever, influenza, poliomyelitis, and measles.
Section A.2.c covers the establishment of bacteriology, verification of germ theory, and identification of disease-producing organisms.
Section A.3.b covers advances in immunology including vaccines for bacterial and viral diseases.
Section A.3.d.ii specifically covers cancer research and the treatment of abnormal cell growths through various therapies.
Section A.3.a covers chemotherapy including antibiotics and synthetic drugs, and B.3 covers pharmacology and experimental therapeutics.
Section A.2.d specifically covers the discovery and use of anesthesia, and A.4.e covers inhalation anesthetic procedures.
Section B.1.g lists psychiatry as a hospital residency specialty, a core field of specialized medical practice.
Medical ethics relates to the charter of medical conduct (A.1.d.ii, Hippocratic tradition) and the ethical dimensions of medical practice.
Section B.2.b covers medical jurisprudence as a clinical specialty, connecting law and medical practice.
Section B.2.d lists public health as a clinical specialty, covering population-level health interventions.
Section A.3.d.i covers nutrition and the treatment of deficiency diseases through vitamins, and B.3 lists nutrition as a nonclinical specialty.
Section A.3.c covers developments in endocrinology including insulin, cortisone, and sex hormones, and B.2.e lists endocrinology as a specialty.
Anatomical discoveries are discussed throughout the history of medicine from ancient times (A.1.d.i, A.1.f.i, A.1.g.i) through Renaissance dissection.
Section A.2.b covers advances in physiology, and B.3 lists medical physiology as a nonclinical specialty.
Neurology (B.1.f) is listed as a hospital residency specialty, and the brain is central to neuroscience in medical practice.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster
mappings.rationale: Sleep medicine relates to neurology (B.1.f) and psychiatry (B.1.g) as specialized medical practice areas.
Pain management connects to anesthesiology (B.1.h) and the historical development of anesthesia (A.2.d).
Section B.3 lists gerontology as a nonclinical specialty and basic medical science.
Dementia relates to neurology (B.1.f) and psychiatry (B.1.g) as specialized areas of medical practice.
Depression relates to psychiatry (B.1.g) as a hospital residency specialty and major area of medical practice.
Schizophrenia is a major psychiatric condition relevant to psychiatry (B.1.g) as a hospital specialization.
Autism relates to neurology (B.1.f), psychiatry (B.1.g), and the study of neurodevelopmental conditions in medicine.
Addiction medicine relates to psychiatry (B.1.g) and pharmacology (B.3) as areas of medical practice.
Psychotherapy is connected to psychiatry (B.1.g) as a therapeutic approach within medical practice.
CBT is a leading therapeutic modality used in psychiatry (B.1.g) and related medical disciplines.
Section C.1 covers the history and practice of dentistry as a discipline affiliated with medicine.
Section A.1.g.i highlights the importance of the microscope to medical studies, including the discovery of blood circulation.
Section A.2.a mentions the cell and cellular pathology as foundational 19th-century medical concepts.
Forensic science connects to medical jurisprudence (B.2.b), pathology (B.1.h), and toxicology (B.2.g).
Veterinary science is a discipline closely affiliated with medicine, sharing many of the same foundational sciences and methods.
Section A.2.e mentions the discovery of X-rays, and B.1.a lists radiology as a hospital specialty using radioactive methods.
Circadian rhythms are relevant to medical physiology (B.3) and the understanding of sleep, hormones, and metabolic disorders.
Dyslexia relates to neurology (B.1.f) and the medical understanding of neurodevelopmental conditions.
Anxiety disorders are a major concern of psychiatry (B.1.g) as a hospital residency specialty.
Psychopathy relates to psychiatry (B.1.g) as a clinical condition studied within medical practice.
